coredata.dc:title: Inhibition of highland barley bran-derived carbon dots on the formation of advanced glycation end products

coredata.dc:description: Advanced glycation end products (AGEs) are among the series factors that cause the pathology of neurodegenerative diseases. Carbon dots (CDs) with low toxicity, tunable surface chemistry and biocompatibility have been used for biological inhibition and as anticancer agents. In this study, CDs derived from highland barley bran (HBB-CDs) were used to inhibit the formation of AGEs in vitro. The possible inhibitory mechanism was attributed to the decreased number of glycosylation sites in glycosylated β-casein from four and six to three and four in the presence of the HBB-CDs, which was revealed by HPLC–Orbitrap tandem mass spectrometry at the molecular level. Furthermore, the glycosylation degree of each glycosylation site was commanded, and the double saccharification capacity of K167 and K214 was changed. Strong inhibition of AGEs formation in the presence of elevated HBB-CDs suggested that CDs are potential anti-glycation agents and food additives against glycation-associated diseases and food processing.

head.ce:title: Inhibition of highland barley bran-derived carbon dots on the formation of advanced glycation end products

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Technology and Business University, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing, 100048, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Technology and Business University, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing, 100048, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Advanced glycation end products (AGEs) are among the series factors that cause the pathology of neurodegenerative diseases. Carbon dots (CDs) with low toxicity, tunable surface chemistry and biocompatibility have been used for biological inhibition and as anticancer agents. In this study, CDs derived from highland barley bran (HBB-CDs) were used to inhibit the formation of AGEs in vitro. The possible inhibitory mechanism was attributed to the decreased number of glycosylation sites in glycosylated β-casein from four and six to three and four in the presence of the HBB-CDs, which was revealed by HPLC–Orbitrap tandem mass spectrometry at the molecular level. Furthermore, the glycosylation degree of each glycosylation site was commanded, and the double saccharification capacity of K167 and K214 was changed. Strong inhibition of AGEs formation in the presence of elevated HBB-CDs suggested that CDs are potential anti-glycation agents and food additives against glycation-associated diseases and food processing.

ce:section.ce:para: A series of chemical reactions between –COOH in reducing sugars and free –NH2 in proteins, lipids or nucleic acids can form the Schiff bases adducts, followed by the generation of Amadori products through Amadori rearrangement. These precursors go through dehydration and rearrangement reactions to produce advanced glycosylation end products (AGEs) (Maillard, 1912; Thorpe & Baynes, 2003). The accumulation of AGEs and excessive formation of oxidative stress in vivo may lead to oxidative and carbonyl stress, inflammation, structural and functional changes of tissue proteins, and it could be a trigger for neurodegenerative diseases (Ashraf et al., 2018). In recent years, growing theory suggests that the level of food-borne AGEs can cause potential health risks associated with AGEs (Baye et al., 2017; Clarke, Dordevic, Tan, Ryan, & Coughlan, 2016; Yu et al., 2021; Zhang, Wang, & Fu, 2020). In the Western diet, the ingestion of food-borne Maillard products was suggested to about 1 g of Amadori reaction products and 25–75 mg of AGEs every day according to the estimation.(Hellwig & Henle, 2014). Therefore, an effective AGEs inhibitor is still urgent to be developed to prevent the human health from AGEs.

In recent years, the most widely reported of food-borne AGEs inhibitors are phenolic compounds, including procyanidins, ferulic acid, gallotannins, and gallic acid (Chen et al., 2019; Khan, Liu, Wang, & Sun, 2020). Furthermore, phenolic compounds derived from plant and cereal extracts, such as corn silk extracts, Baijiu vinasse extracts, Alpinia zerumbet rhizomes, and Astragalus radix, are commonly used as inhibitors (Chompoo, Upadhyay, Kishimoto, Makise, & Tawata, 2011; Motomura et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2019; Zhang, Wang, & Liu, 2020). Highland barley bran is good output in Qinghai province of China, which is reported to contain a large number of phenolic compounds (Shen et al., 2016; Siebenhandl et al., 2007). This crop occupies the largest planting area and the highest field yield, because it is the only crop that can be planted at a high altitude of 4200–4500 m above sea level (Liu, Yao, Yu, & Zhong, 2013). Highland barley bran extracts have been used to inhibit AGEs in our laboratory, producing an inhibition effect of 45.58% in vitro (H. Liu, Chen, et al., 2018). Although natural extracts have advantages of structural diversity, few side effects, and high inhibitory activity in vitro, some problems that cannot be ignored including low extraction rates, complicated extraction procedure, inadequate solubility, furthermore the possibility of organic solvent residues is a non-negligible problem (Du, Tu, Wang, & Hu, 2020; Yu et al., 2015). Therefore, the identification of more suitable AGEs inhibitors is important.

Nanomaterials have a bright future because of their particular properties, including simple composition, large surface area, great biocompatibility, easy surface modification, and multifunctional combination with biomolecules (Berdimurodov, Verma, Kholikov, Akbarov, & Guo, 2021; Zhang, Xu, Liu, & Sun, 2022; Zhang, Zhu, Li, Liu, & Sun, 2022). Nanomaterials have facilitated great progress in biomedicine, including disease diagnosis, treatment, and prevention, to promote human health (Farokhzad & Langer, 2009). Selenium, gold, and zinc oxide nanoparticles have been used to inhibit AGEs and oxidative modifications, with the results showing that these nanoparticles are potential tools for counteracting oxidative stress and inhibiting protein glycosylation in vitro (Lim, Shen, & Gao, 2015; Seneviratne, Narayanan, Liu, & Dain, 2012). Carbon dots (CDs) as a novel nanomaterial have received significant attention owing to their low cost, stability, sustainability, simple synthetic routes, tunable surface chemistry, biocompatibility, and low toxicity, which are essential requirements for a promising inhibitor (Boobalan et al., 2020; Cao et al., 2021). CDs have been used as high-efficiency inhibitors of human insulin fibrillation, and as antibacterial and anticancer agents (Li et al., 2015). However, the potential applications of CDs in inhibiting protein glycosylation and decreasing AGEs levels have rarely been investigated.

In this study, CDs were used to inhibit glycosylation of the β-casein and d-glucose system. We selected three materials containing a variety of phenolic compounds to synthesize biomass CDs, which obtained from highland barley bran, naked oats, and grape seeds. These three biomass CDs named as HBB-CDs, NO-CDs and GS-CDs, respectively, and the inhibition effect of them on the glycosylation was determined by detecting the constitution of fluorescent and nonfluorescent AGEs, typically pentosidine and pyrrolidine, changes in the free amino acids of glycosylated β-casein, and some other variation in the structure and molecular weight (MW) of glycosylated proteins. The glycosylation sites of glycosylated β-casein were identified by high performance liquid chromatography–Orbitrap tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC−Orbitrap−MS/MS) to discuss the inhibition of CDs on the glycosylation at the molecular level, and the possibility inhibition mechanism of CDs to AGEs formation was deeply discussed. Biomass HBB-CDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method, and characterized by TEM, FT-IR, and XPS analysis. In conclusion, this paper is helpful for understanding the mechanism of CDs inhibiting AGEs formation and can provide a feasible strategy for the application of CDs as new antiglycation products and novel food additives candidate in food processing.

β-casein (C6905), d-glucose (B88872), serine, o-phthalaldehyde (OPA), pentosidine, pyrraline, sodium dodecyl sulfate, dithiothreitol, and acetonitrile were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Highland barley bran, grape seed, and naked oats were provided by a local factory. The other reagents were purchased from J&K Scientific Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM; SU8020, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) was used to observe the morphology of CDs. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), a multifunctional enzyme labeling instrument (MELI; SYNERGY HI, Biotek, US) was used to analyze the fluorescence properties of CDs. Particle size analysis software (Nano Measurer 1.2, UK) was applied to measure the particle size distribution of CDs. MALDI-TOF/TOF (RapifleX, Bruker, US) was used to determine the MW of proteins in analog systems. HPLC−Orbitrap−MS/MS (Thermo Fisher, US) was used to determine glycosylation sites. Ultraviolet Spectrophotometry (Agilent, Cary 100 UV–Vis, Beijing, CN) was used to determine the content of free amino acid.

Fluorescent CDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method. Processed highland barley bran, naked oats, or grape seeds (7 g) were added in distilled water (60 mL) with stirring. The mixed solution was added to a 100 mL Teflon-lined high-temperature reaction kettle, heated at 200 °C for 3 h, then cooled to room temperature. The products were filtered and the obtained filtrate was further centrifuged for 15 min at 12,000 rpm to remove larger agglomerated nanoparticles. To further remove remaining particles, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and conducted through a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 48 h (changing deionized water every 3 h). Finally, the obtained light brown CDs suspension was dried by rotary evaporation, and the obtained crystalline solid was ground to obtain fluorescent CDs powder.

Fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) were used to evaluate the luminescence of CDs according to a reported method (Kumari, Kumar, Sahu, & Kumar, 2018). Quinine sulfate (QY = 56% in 0.1 M H2SO4) was selected as the reference for CDs. Then the QYs were calculated using the following equation:
(1) QY = Φ R × (F
X /F
R )(A
R /A
X )(η
R
/η
X )
Where Φ is the QY, and F is the integral of the fluorescence intensity. A is the ultraviolet absorbance at optimum wavelength, η is the refractive index; X and R represent the targets and reference substances, respectively.

A mixture of β-casein and d-glucose was dissolved in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) after adding CDs. The final concentrations of β-casein, d-glucose, and CDs were 150, 150, and 10 mg/mL, respectively. Then the compound was heated at 40 °C for 24 h, and stored at 4–8 °C for next experimental study. A blank group, comprising β-casein and d-glucose without CDs, was used in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of total fluorescent products of AGEs and pentosidine
ce:section.ce:para: The total fluorescent products of AGEs and pentosidine were detected using a MELI (Zhang et al., 2019). The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were set to 350 nm and 425 nm for the total fluorescent products of AGEs, and 335 nm and 382 nm for pentosidine, respectively. After adding 200 μL samples to a 96-well plate, the mixtures were detected by the MELI.

Antioxidant tests were performed using 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and 2,2′-azino-bis-(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS·
+ ) to verify the oxidation resistance of CDs. First, the CDs solution (2 mg/mL) was added into a 2 mL of DPPH solution (2 mmol/L soluble in ethanol) and mixed for 2 h in the dark (Espín, Soler-Rivas, & Wichers, 2000), and the UV absorbance was measured at 517 nm after the reaction. The DPPH inhibition effects were calculated by the following equation:
(2) % DPPH inhibition = [1−((A
1 −A 2)/A
3 )] × 100%
Where A
1 is the measured UV absorbance of the experimental group, A
2 is the UV absorbance measured for anhydrous ethanol solution containing only DPPH, and A
3 is the UV absorbance of the blank group.

0.35 mL 7.4 mmol/L ABTS·
+ and 0.35 mL 2.6 mmol/L K2S2O8 was mixed for 12 h under dark conditions to prepare ABTS·
+ diluent, and ABTS·
+ working fluid was obtained by diluting ABTS·
+ diluent 40 times with a pH = 7.4 phosphoric acid buffer solution (Marfil et al., 2011). The CDs solution (2 mg/mL, 0.2 mL) was then added to the ABTS·
+ working solution (0.8 mL), and the solution was vibrated for 10s by vortex oscillator and allowed to stand for 6 min. The ABTS·
+ inhibition rate was calculated by the following equation:
(3) % ABTS·
+ inhibition = ((A
0 −A))/A
0 × 100%
Where A is the UV absorbance at 734 nm, A
0 is the UV absorbance of 95% anhydrous ethanol measured at 734 nm.

Free amino acid was detected by ultraviolet spectrophotometry (Vigo, Malec, Gomez, & Llosa, 1992). OPA (0.150 g) was dissolved in methanol (1 mL), and dithiothreitol (0.166 g) was dissolved in ultrapure water (2 mL). The sample (50 μL, 2 mg/mL) was then blended with the OPA reagent (1 mL), and the absorbance at 340 nm was confirmed after in the dark for 2 min. Each sample treatments were performed in triplicate.

SDS-PAGE was conducted using the SDS-PAGE Gel Kit (Solarbio, P1200, Beijing, CN), according to the method of Zhang et al. (Zhang et al., 2018). These sample were fully mixed with the loading buffer, then the resulting mixture was heated at 95 °C for 5 min. The samples (10 μL) treated above were added to the pre-configured gel which consisting of 5% septum gel also 12% separated gel. The electrophoresis was performed at 80 V for 30 min and then adjusted to 120 V for 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time of flight mass spectrometry (MALDI−TOF MS) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: MW study of the impact of CDs on β-casein was conducted at 40 °C for 0–24 h by MALDI-TOF MS (BRKR, Karlsruhe, DE) in positive ion mode. An appropriate amount of the sample was dissolved in 50% acetonitrile aqueous solution (0.1% TFA) by shaking, then diluted to three different concentrations. The sample solution (0.5 μL) was placed on a 384-well MALDI target plate, and allowed to dry naturally at room temperature. A α-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid (HCCA) matrix solution (0.5 μL, 0.5 g/L in 50% acetonitrile aqueous solution containing 0.1% TFA) was then added and allowed to dry naturally at room temperature. The laser source was a 335-nm 3D Smartbeam Nd:YAG (355 mm) laser, with the Smartbeam parameter set to M5 Small. Spectrogram acquisition accumulated 500 excitations at a laser frequency of 10 kHz and baseline elimination was performed. Automatic data acquisition mode was used to collect data.

The specimen was first disinfected by SDS-PAGE. When slaking with trypsin buffer at 37 °C for 16 h, glycosylation β-casein was separated by HPLC (EASY-nLC 1200) armed with a RP-C18 column (150 μm × 15 cm, Thermo Fisher). The gradient elution program was as follows: 4%–8% B over 2 min, 8%–28% B over 43 min, 28%–40% B over 10 min, 40%–95% B over 1 min, and 95% B held for 10 min. The raw MS files were analyzed and searched against a target protein database based on the sample species using Byonic. The parameters were set as follows: Protein modifications, carbamidomethylation (C) (fixed), oxidation (M) (variable), monoglycation (K) (variable), dual-glycation (K) (variable), and triglycation (K) (variable); enzyme specificity, trypsin; maximum missed cleavages, 3; precursor ion mass tolerance, 20 ppm, and MS/MS tolerance, 0.02 Da.

All data were conducted in triplicate, and the results were expressed as mean values ± standard deviation (SD). The statistical differences of data were analyzed by one-way analysis using SPSS software version 17.0. p < 0.05 was considered as a statistical significance. The figures were drawn by Origin 2018 64Bit software.

The successful preparation of biomass CDs was conducted using naked oats, highland barley bran, and grape seeds as the raw materials by one-step hydrothermal synthesis (Fig. S1). Naked oats are the annual herbs, whose fibrous roots often with sand jacket. Highland barley bran are the seed coat of wheat obtained after grinding flour. And the grape seeds are obtained by drying and separating from the grape skins and stalks. These materials are cheap, readily available and can be harnessed to make the non-toxic CDs that we need. The optical properties of the three biomass CDs could be seen in Fig. S1. The ultraviolet absorption spectrum of NO-CDs (Fig. S1A (a)) exhibited a vigorous absorption peak at 285 nm, attributing to the n–π* transition of the CO bands of carboxyl groups on the exterior of NO-CDs, which was consistent with carbon nanoparticles synthesized by the carbonization of citric acid, ascorbic acid, etc (Dong et al., 2014; Lu et al., 2012; Zou et al., 2014). When the excitation wavelength progressively increased, the maximum emission peak was shifted to a longer wavelength. Under the excitation of 350 nm, a bright blue light centered at 420 nm could be observed (Fig. S1A (b) and (c)). This unique phenomenon of excitation dependent photoluminescence proved that the reaction products must be NO-CDs, which was also in agreement with optical properties of those CDs that were prepared by other groups (Lu et al., 2015; Yin et al., 2013). The inset (right) of Fig. S1A showed the photograph of the NO-CDs under UV light illumination, presenting a typical blue-green luminescence. As shown in Fig. S1B (a), the UV absorption spectrum of the HBB-CDs exhibited the absorption peak at 285 nm and 345 nm, attributing to the n–π* transition of the CO bands of carboxyl groups on the surface of HBB-CDs and the surface modification, respectively. Due to the ingredient's complexity of highland barley bran, HBB-CDs was further analyzed by structural characterization. The fluorescence emission spectrum of HBB-CDs showed an obvious peak at 450 nm with the optimum excitation wavelength of 350 nm (Fig. S1B (b) and (c)). The inset (right) of Fig. S1B showed the color of the HBB-CDs under UV light illumination, presenting a typical blue-green luminescence. The UV absorption spectrum of GS-CDs exhibited a vigorous absorption peak at 285 nm, assigning to the n–π* transition of CO bands of carboxyl groups on the surface of GS-CDs (Fig. S1C (a)). The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of GS-CDs were 330 and 380 nm, respectively (Fig. S1C (b) and (c)). All the three CDs showed blue-green luminescence under UV light illumination, but under visible light, GS-CDs showed brownish yellow luminescence and lower definition than the other two CDs (Fig. S1D). Furthermore, the QY values of HBB-CDs, NO-CDs and GS-CDs were 17.4%, 16.2%, and 12.5%, respectively, measured by using quinine sulfate as the standard (Table S1). The HBB-CDs with highest QY might be ascribed to the particular chemical nature and compositions of highland barley bran whose abundant surface functional groups introduced more defect emissive sites and calcium (Ca) doping onto CDs (Fig. 1 F). These were comparable with those of reported luminescent CDs, showing that the as-prepared CDs had good luminescent properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibitory effect of biomass CDs on the formation of AGEs
ce:section.ce:para: The inhibitory effect of biomass CDs on the total fluorescent products of AGEs and pentosidine were investigated. It could be seen from Fig. S2A that with the time prolonged, the total fluorescent products of AGEs gradually increased. But compared with the blank group, the fluorescence intensity of formed AGEs in the presence of all three biomass CDs significant decreased, in which HBB-CDs group with lowest intensity indicated its best inhibitory effect to the formation of AGEs (inhibition of 33%). Similarly, the formation of pentosidine, a typical fluorescent product of AGEs, was gradually increased within 24 h during β-casein glycosylation (Fig. S2B). The inhibition effects of the three biomass CDs on pentosidine were consistent with those on the total fluorescent products of AGEs. The HBB-CDs showed the strongest inhibition effect on pentosidine, with inhibition ratio of 39.06% which were used in subsequent investigations. The above results proved that CDs could not completely block the generation of AGEs during the glycation process but could significantly reduce AGEs production during glycation. Research focused on the inhibition of AGEs formation in recent years were summarized in Table 1 from the inhibitors used, mechanism and inhibition (%). It can be seen from Table 1 that materials with rich phenolic compounds can be used to inhibit the formation of AGEs. Notably, biomass CDs derived from highland barley bran exhibited the satisfactory inhibition of AGEs that could be comparable or even better than that of in reported study, which demonstrated the possibility of HBB-CDs as AGEs inhibitor. To understand the effects of nanoscale HBB-CDs on the inhibition of AGEs, the specific mechanism was further investigated.

TEM analysis showed that HBB-CDs was mainly spherical and monodisperse (Fig. 1A). And the average particle sizes were estimated to be approximate 10 nm, as displayed in Fig. 1A. Fig. 1B showed the fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of HBB-CDs, and changes were observed in the characteristic peaks at 3376 cm−1, which was assigned to the phenolic hydroxyl group of HBB-CDs and the aliphatic side chain of the protein. The peak appeared at 2877 cm−1, which was assigned to C–H vibrations of methylene groups, while peaks at 1651 cm−1 demonstrated the presence of CO. The characteristic peaks of HBB-CDs at 1361 cm−1and 1517 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of C–O, and the peak at 1020 cm−1 was putted down to Ca–O bending vibrations. Therefore, it could be concluded that the synthesized HBB-CDs were functionalized by hydroxyl and carboxylic/carbonyl moieties, which originated from carbohydrates present in the highland barley bran. The presence of these functional groups imparted the excellent solubility of HBB-CDs in water without any further chemical modification. These functional groups determined by FT-IR spectroscopy were in good agreement with the XPS results. XPS analysis showed that the HBB-CDs was mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and calcium (Fig. 1C), with relative contents of 62.18%, 30.47%, 4.12%, and 2.6%, respectively, in which the content of oxygen was the highest. The high oxygen content demonstrated that the HBB-CDs was functionalized by the abundant CO and C–O groups. HBB-CDs with higher oxygen contents had been reported to show an excellent inhibitory effect on protein fibrillation (Li et al., 2019). The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum exhibited characteristic CC/C–C, C–N, C–O, and CO peaks at 284.6, 285.5, 286.6, and 288.2 eV, respectively (Fig. 1D). Furthermore, high-resolution N1s peaks were observed at around 399.7 and 401.8 eV, which were assigned to N–H and C–N groups, respectively (Fig. 1E). The appearance of the N1s peak indicated that nitrogen had been successfully incorporated into the carbon framework. The O1s XPS spectrum was decomposed into peaks at 531.7, 532.9, and 534.2 eV, corresponding to Ca–O, C–O, and CO groups, respectively (Fig. 1F). The Ca2p XPS spectrum was decomposed into peaks at 347.5 and 351.2 eV, corresponding to Ca–O groups (Fig. 1G). Highland barley bran contained a large amount of calcium, which might be remained after the carbonization process. These results were consistent with the previous characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultraviolet absorption and fluorescence of glycosylated β-casein
ce:section.ce:para: Tryptophan (Trp) was primarily responsible for the changing of UV absorption of β-casein. Glycosylation usually expands the construction of proteins and exposes tryptophan remnants to the exterior of protein molecules, resulting in the increasing of the absorbance. UV absorption spectra of glycosylated β-casein without and with HBB-CDs were presented in Fig. 2 A and B, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2A, the absorbance of glycosylation β-casein without HBB-CDs increased with the extension of response time while the absorbance of glycosylation β-casein with HBB-CDs declined faintly at 0–9 h and slowly enhanced at 9–24 h (Fig. 2B). Different change in the absorbance of glycochemical system showed that the addition of HBB-CDs inhibited the glycosylation of β-casein. The inherent fluorescence intensity of β-casein was mostly putted down to Trp157 (Sofronova, Semenyuk, & Muronetz, 2019), and the change of fluorescence intensity could be used to reflect the conformational changes of the tertiary structure of protein. As shown in Fig. 2C, with the extension of response time, the fluorescence intensity of β-casein was rapidly decreased, which indicated that β-casein was glycosylated and the tertiary structure of protein was changed. The decreasing of fluorescence intensity might be on account of the slow expansion of the conformation structure of proteins during glycosylation, exposing aromatic residues to hydrophilic solvents. In contrast, after adding the HBB-CDs into the system, the fluorescence intensity of β-casein exhibited a slowly decreasing trend, which demonstrated that the addition of HBB-CDs played a vital role in the conversion of β-casein to be glycosylated. And the reason for why fluorescence intensity of glycochemical system decreased was further investigated. As shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescence intensity of native β-casein decreased after adding the HBB-CDs, indicating that HBB-CDs could quench the fluorescence intensity of β-casein. Considering the various degrees of fluorescence intensity attenuation, different glycosylation sites were believed to occur between glycosylated β-caseins in the presence of HBB-CDs, and the glycosylation sites also differed at various response times.

Proteins were covalently linked to glucose through amino residues and aldehyde groups, which increased MW and advanced protein polymerization (like dimer or trimer formation). The SDS-PAGE outlines of glycosylated β-casein with and without HBB-CDs were displayed in Fig. 3 A and B. In comparison to native β-casein (0 h), the MW of glycosylation β-casein raised slowly with an extended response time. The increase of MW in Fig. 3B was significantly better than that in Fig. 3A, indicating that the addition of HBB-CDs inhibited the Maillard reaction between protein and glucose, which resulted in the slowly increase of MW. These phenomena explained that d-glucose had formed a covalent bond with β-casein, and the formation of new covalent bonds increased with the spread of the response time. The addition of HBB-CDs inhibited the covalent binding between protein and glucose, so the inhibition effect of HBB-CDs on glycosylation reaction was judged by the increase of MW.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of antioxidant capacity of HBB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Nanomaterials had remarkable free radical scavenging ability and have shown excellent positive effects in the field of nanomedicine (Li et al., 2017). HBB-CDs as one of emerging nanomaterials had the ability of scavenging hydroxyl radicals, which was related to the concentration of HBB-CDs. Smrithi and his partner assessed the antioxidant activity of b-CDs which synthesized from Beta vulgaris by DPPH method and found the scavenging activity of b-CDs reached the maximum at 1000 μg/mL, indicating that b-CDs could pair single electrons and reduce the absorption wavelength (Smrithi et al., 2022). Therefore, HBB-CDs might have the ability to scavenge DPPH free radicals. The antioxidant activity of HBB-CDs was determined by measuring the DPPH and ABTS·
+ scavenging abilities. As shown in Fig. 3C, the DPPH free radical scavenging activity was 60.8%, while the ABTS·
+ free radical scavenging activity was 41.3%. The satisfying results showed that the HBB-CDs exhibited good antioxidant capacity, which may attribute to the surface groups of HBB-CDs, such as hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the free amino acid
ce:section.ce:para: Usually, we regarded the content of free amino acids as an indicator to evaluate the degree of proteins being glycosylated. Thus, in the following experiments, the effect of HBB-CDs on the formation of glycosylated β-casein at different reaction times was investigated by using serine content as indicator (G. Liu, Chen, et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. S4, the linear equation of serine was y = 1.3539x + 0.0688, with an R2 value of 0.9969. As shown in Fig. 3D, the content of free amino acids in glycosylation samples containing HBB-CDs had basically declined, but the decline was not obvious, especially at 0–12 h. This indicated that HBB-CDs somewhat slowed the glycosylation while could not completely block glycosylation. Similar consequences were established in examine of Bodiga et al., which showed that β-carotene prevented the decline in accessible amino acid in diastatic albumin from bovine serum (Bodiga et al., 2013). These consequences only indicated that HBB-CDs had a definite repressive effect on the glycosylation process, however, how HBB-CDs affected the protein glycosylation sites and the degree of glycosylation at all glycosylation site remained unknown. Thus, to acquire elaborate information regarding glycosylated β-casein with and without HBB-CDs, accurate identification of the protein glycosylation sites was conducted using HPLC–Orbitrap-MS/MS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of glycosylated peptides and glycosylation sites
ce:section.ce:para: The glycosylation sites of 12 and 24 h of β-casein glycosylated with and without HBB-CDs were explored through HPLC-Orbitrap-MS/MS. For the purpose of obtain MS/MS spectrum, a higher-energy collision dissociation (HCD) atomization pattern was confirmed. In addition, the impact of HBB-CDs on the MW of β-casein was investigated by MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry. As shown in Fig. S5, the MW of β-casein was 26758.7 Da, while the MW of β-casein reacted with HBB-CDs at 0, 6, 12 and 24 h at 40 °C was 26758.7, 26758.5, 26758.6 and 26758.3 Da, respectively, which demonstrated that no chemical response and covalent bonding formed between HBB-CDs and β-casein. Thus, HBB-CDs would not affect the location of glycosylated peptides and glycosylation sites in glycosylated β-casein digested through trypsin.

Then the accurate sequence and glycosylation sites of polypeptides were verified by HCD MS/MS spectrum combined with neutral loss detection technology. Theoretically, if the peptide is monosaccharified by d-glucose, the mass will increase by 162.058 Da. Therefore, a m/z offset of 81.029 occurred when the charge was 2. Fig. 4 showed the MS/MS spectrum of typical β-casein glycosylated peptide in the sequence of 213–220 after interacting with HBB-CDs for 24 h. Unglycosylated peptide 213TKVIPYVR220 was observed at m/z 488.32+, while its glycosylated forms were observed at m/z 569.3292+ and 650.3562+, with mass shifts of 162.058 and 324.106 Da, indicating that peptides 213–220 might subsist in monosaccharizated and double saccharification forms (Fig. 4A). Fig. 4B showed the HCD MS/MS spectrum of the monoglycosylated peptide, in which the peak at m/z 569.3292+ demonstrated a series of b and y ions (b ion was at the top of sequence and y ion was at the bottom of sequence). The glycosylation site, K214, was determined by the mass variance between b6 and b7 or between y1 and y2, which was the blended mass of lysine and the d-glucose residue. This monosaccharizated form was ordinary, while double saccharification of a glycosylation site was unusual. In the HCD MS/MS spectrometry of double saccharification peptide 213TKVIPYVR220, a peak at m/z 650.3562+ (Fig. 4C), a battery of matching b and y ions affirmed K214 was blended with two d-glucose molecules. It could be seen that this consequence accorded with monosaccharization (schematic diagram shown in Fig. 5 E), in other words, a condensation reaction occurred between the amino acid group of the protein and reduction of sugar carbonyl groups. Previous studies have ignored the occurrence of disaccharification. This might be focused on in the future study. Glycosylation sites primarily appeared on the guanidine group of protein N-end amino group, lysine residual amino group and arginine residual group. Table 2 showed the dynamic changes of glycosylation site of glycosylated β-casein at different reaction time. Glycosylated β-casein with HBB-CDs were three (K167, K180 and K214) and four (K167, K180, K206 and K214) glycosylation sites in 12 h and 24 h, respectively. Among them, glycosylation sites K167 and K214 underwent bisaccharization. Correspondingly, the blank groups had four (K91, K167, K180, and K214) and six (K39, K91, K167, K180, K206, and K214) glycosylation sites at 12 and 24 h, respectively. It was obvious that the number of glycosylation sites was reduced after the addition of HBB-CDs. The previous studies proved that both HBB-CDs addition and the glycosylation response time affected quantity of glycosylation sites. HBB-CDs could significantly reduce the number of glycosylation sites, meaning that HBB-CDs would hinder the progress of glycation reactions, thereby reducing the production of AGEs. In addition, the binding sites of protein and monosaccharide were specific. Although the time of the glycosylation reaction had a certain influence on the number of glycosylation sites, the glycosylation sites were highly reproducible. Yang et al. found that a different power of ultrasonic treatment of glycosylation protein would significantly affect the DSP value of the protein glycosylation site but barely affect the glycosylation site changes (Yang et al., 2017). These results suggested HPLC-Orbitrap-MS/MS was an effective way to study the molecular mechanism of HBB-CDs on glycosylation.

The distribution maps of glycosylation sites under different conditions were shown in Fig. 5A–D. As shown in Fig. 5A and C, four and six glycosylation sites were observed without HBB-CDs at 12 h and 24 h, respectively. When HBB-CDs were added, the glycosylation sites were decreased to three and four, respectively (Fig. 5B and D). Obviously, two new glycosylation sites, K39 and K206, appeared in glycosylated β-casein without HBB-CDs after 24 h. Furthermore, two highly active glycosylation sites of K167 and K214 were found. These two glycosylation sites were merged by two d-glucose molecules, and the response plan displayed in Fig. 5F showed that monosaccharide could produce four types of disaccharide peptide with d-ribose molecules. K167 and K214 were the most reactive glycosylation site, with only the dual-glycation phenomenon occurring, which could be found in all glycated samples. But in some other reports, for example, Du et al. discovered that double saccharification site of K91 was found in glycated β-Lg with/without selenium nanoparticles (SeNPs) after 12 h (Du et al., 2020). These findings showed that the double saccharification of different glycosylation sites occurred gradually with the deepening of the degree of the glycation reaction. Therefore, the reduction of AGEs contents in the glycation process might relate to the ability of HBB-CDs to change the double saccharification of each glycosylation site. That is, the inhibition effect of HBB-CDs on the formation of AGEs may be attributed to reducing the number of glycosylation sites via blocking the glycosylation process.

In summary, biomass HBB-CDs with low toxicity and high antioxidant activities were used to inhibit the formation of AGEs in vitro. HPLC–Orbitrap–MS/MS analysis showed that, after 12 and 24 h in the presence of HBB-CDs, the number of glycosylation sites in glycosylated β-casein decreased from four and six to three and four, respectively. The glycosylation degree of every glycosylation site was controlled, and the double saccharification ability of K167 and K214 was changed. The fresh antiglycation properties of HBB-CDs might play a curative role in glycosylation-related diseases in year to come. The restraint of glycosylation by HBB-CDs is interesting and provides a reference for future antiglycation studies on nanomaterials for the prevention of AGEs formation in vivo.

Pei Zhu: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Ying Zhang: Writing – original draft, wrote and revised the manuscript, Writing – original draft. Chenchen Xie: Validation, Data curation, Methodology. Huilin Liu: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Baoguo Sun: Supervision, Project administration.
